{{Short description|Anti-democratic, reactionary philosophy founded by Curtis Yarvin in 2007}}
{{Distinguish|Counter-Enlightenment|Intellectual dark web}}
The '''Dark Enlightenment''', also called the '''neo-reactionary movement''' (sometimes abbreviated to '''NRx'''), is an [[anti-democratic]], [[anti-egalitarian]],<ref>{{cite book |last1=Kindinger |first1=Evangelia |last2=Schmitt |first2=Mark |title=The Intersections of Whiteness |date=4 January 2019 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-11277-2 |url=https://www.google.co.uk/books/edition/The_Intersections_of_Whiteness/b3uCDwAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=%22Dark+Enlightenment%22+anti-egalitarian&pg=PT209&printsec=frontcover |access-date=29 November 2022 |language=en |chapter=Conclusion: Digital culture and the afterlife of white supremacist movements}}</ref> [[reactionary]] philosophical and political movement. In 2007 and 2008, software engineer [[Curtis Yarvin]], writing under the [[pen name]] Mencius Moldbug, articulated what would develop into Dark Enlightenment thinking. Yarvin's theories were elaborated and expanded by philosopher [[Nick Land]], who first coined the term Dark Enlightenment in his essay of the same name. The term "Dark Enlightenment" is a reaction to the [[Age of Enlightenment]].<ref name="TechCrunch: Geeks for Monarchy"/><ref name="SPLC">{{cite web|first=Jon|last=Phillips|url=https://secure.splcenter.org/get-informed/intelligence-report/browse-all-issues/2014/fall/Troublesome-Sources|title=Troublesome Sources|website=Southern Poverty Law Center|date=Fall 2014|access-date=2015-02-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150224084216/https://secure.splcenter.org/get-informed/intelligence-report/browse-all-issues/2014/fall/Troublesome-Sources|archive-date=2015-02-24|url-status=dead}}</ref>

The ideology generally rejects [[Whig history|Whig historiography]]<ref name="qz" />—the concept that history shows an inevitable progression towards greater [[liberty]] and enlightenment, culminating in [[liberal democracy]] and [[constitutional monarchy]]<ref name="qz" />—in favor of a return to traditional societal constructs and forms of government, including [[Absolute monarchy|absolute monarchism]] and other older forms of leadership such as [[cameralism]].<ref name=behind>{{cite news |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2017/02/behind-the-internets-dark-anti-democracy-movement/516243/ |title=Behind the Internet's Anti-Democracy Movement |date=10 February 2017 |website=The Atlantic |last=Gray |first=Rosie |access-date=15 February 2017 |archive-date=10 February 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170210123306/https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2017/02/behind-the-internets-dark-anti-democracy-movement/516243/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In July 2010, [[Arnold Kling]], an adjunct scholar at the [[Cato Institute]], coined the term "neo-reactionaries" to describe Yarvin and his followers.<ref name="TechCrunch: Geeks for Monarchy" />

== Overview <!-- 'Neocameralism' redirects here --> ==
Neo-reactionaries are an informal community of bloggers and political theorists who have been active since the 2000s. [[Steve Sailer]] and [[Hans-Hermann Hoppe]] are contemporary forerunners of the ideology, which also draws influence from philosophers such as [[Thomas Carlyle]] and [[Julius Evola]].<ref name="TechCrunch: Geeks for Monarchy">{{cite web |url=https://techcrunch.com/2013/11/22/geeks-for-monarchy/ |website=TechCrunch |title=Geeks for Monarchy: The Rise of the Neoreactionaries |date=22 November 2013 |first=Klint |last=Finley |access-date=25 June 2017 |archive-date=26 March 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140326035842/http://techcrunch.com/2013/11/22/geeks-for-monarchy/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Central to Land's ideas is a belief in freedom's incompatibility with [[democracy]]. Land drew inspiration from [[libertarian]]s such as [[Peter Thiel]], as indicated in his essay ''The Dark Enlightenment''.<ref name=land>{{cite web|url=http://www.thedarkenlightenment.com/the-dark-enlightenment-by-nick-land/|title=The Dark Enlightenment|author=Land, Nick|author-link=Nick Land|website=The Dark Enlightmenent|access-date=2014-12-23|archive-date=2013-09-25|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130925165535/http://www.thedarkenlightenment.com/the-dark-enlightenment-by-nick-land/|url-status=live}}</ref>{{non-primary source needed|date=October 2022}} The Dark Enlightenment has been described by journalists and commentators as [[alt-right]] and [[Neo-fascism|neo-fascist]].<ref name="qz">{{cite news |first1=Olivia |last1=Goldhill |access-date=2018-05-27 |title=The neo-fascist philosophy that underpins both the alt-right and Silicon Valley technophiles |url=https://qz.com/1007144/the-neo-fascist-philosophy-that-underpins-both-the-alt-right-and-silicon-valley-technophiles/ |newspaper=Quartz |archive-date=2017-06-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170618123305/https://qz.com/1007144/the-neo-fascist-philosophy-that-underpins-both-the-alt-right-and-silicon-valley-technophiles/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="vocativ">{{cite web |url=http://www.vocativ.com/12-2013/dark-enlightenment-creepy-internet-movement-youd-better-take-seriously/ |archive-date=2013-12-17 |date=2 December 2013 |first=Matt |last=Sigl |work=Vocativ |title=The Dark Enlightenment: The Creepy Internet Movement You'd Better Take Seriously |access-date=2016-06-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131217072334/http://www.vocativ.com/12-2013/dark-enlightenment-creepy-internet-movement-youd-better-take-seriously/ |url-status=dead}}</ref> A 2016 article in ''[[New York (magazine)|New York]]'' magazine notes that "Neoreaction has a number of different strains, but perhaps the most important is a form of post-libertarian [[futurism]] that, realizing that libertarians aren't likely to win any elections, argues against democracy in favor of [[Authoritarianism|authoritarian]] forms of government."<ref>{{cite web |url=http://nymag.com/selectall/2016/06/peter-thiel.html |title=Why Peter Thiel Wants to Topple Gawker and Elect Donald Trump |author=MacDougald, Park |date=14 June 2016 |work=New York Magazine |access-date=9 October 2017 |archive-date=5 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181005100205/http://nymag.com/selectall/2016/06/peter-thiel.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

[[Andy Beckett]] stated that "NRx" supporters "believe in the replacement of modern nation-states, democracy and government bureaucracies by authoritarian city states, which on neoreaction blogs sound as much like idealised medieval kingdoms as they do modern enclaves such as [[Singapore]]."<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/may/11/accelerationism-how-a-fringe-philosophy-predicted-the-future-we-live-in | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220411030541/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/may/11/accelerationism-how-a-fringe-philosophy-predicted-the-future-we-live-in | archive-date=2022-04-11 | title=Accelerationism: How a fringe philosophy predicted the future we live in &#124; Philosophy &#124; the Guardian | website=[[TheGuardian.com]] }}</ref>

According to criminal justice professor George Michael, neoreaction seeks to save its ideal of [[Western culture|Western civilization]] through adoption of a monarchical, or [[CEO]] model of government to replace democracy. It also embraces the notion of "[[accelerationism|acceleration]]", first articulated by [[Vladimir Lenin]] as "worse is better", but in the neoreaction version, the creation and promotion of ever more societal crises hastens the adoption of the neoreactive state instead of a communist one.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Michael |first=George |title=An antidemocratic philosophy called 'neoreaction' is creeping into GOP politics |url=http://theconversation.com/an-antidemocratic-philosophy-called-neoreaction-is-creeping-into-gop-politics-182581 |access-date=2022-07-28 |website=The Conversation |language=en}}</ref>

Other focuses of neoreaction often include an idealization of physical fitness; a rationalist or utilitarian justification for social stratification based on intelligence, based on either heredity or meritocracy; an embrace of [[Classical antiquity|Classical]] philosophy, and traditional gender roles.{{Citation needed|date=April 2021}}

When approached by ''[[The Atlantic]]'' political affairs reporter [[Rosie Gray]], Yarvin attempted to troll her on Twitter, and blogger Nick B. Steves said that her IQ was inadequate to the task of interviewing him and that, as a journalist, she was "the enemy".<ref name=behind/>

By mid-2017, NRx had moved to forums such as the Social Matter online forum, the Hestia Society, and Thermidor Magazine. 

In 2021, Yarvin appeared on Fox News' "[[Tucker Carlson|Tucker Carlson Today]]", where he discussed the United States' [[Withdrawal of United States troops from Afghanistan (2020–2021)|withdrawal from Afghanistan]] and his concept of the "Cathedral", which he claims to be the current aggregation of political power and influential institutions that is controlling the country.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Conservative blogger Curtis Yarvin joins 'Tucker Carlson Today'|url=http://video.foxnews.com/v/6271592770001/|access-date=2022-01-31|website=Fox News|language=en}}</ref>

== Criticism ==
Journalist [[Andrew Sullivan]] argues that neoreaction's pessimistic appraisal of democracy dismisses many advances that have been made and that global manufacturing patterns also limit the economic independence that sovereign states can have from one another.<ref>{{cite web|author=Sullivan, Andrew|date=30 April 2017|title=Why the reactionary right must be taken seriously|url=http://nymag.com/daily/intelligencer/2017/04/andrew-sullivan-why-the-reactionary-right-must-be-taken-seriously.html|publisher=New York Magazine|access-date=14 October 2017|archive-date=8 October 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181008140723/http://nymag.com/daily/intelligencer/2017/04/andrew-sullivan-why-the-reactionary-right-must-be-taken-seriously.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In an article for ''[[The Sociological Review]]'', after an examination of neoreaction's core tenets, Roger Burrows deplores the ideology as "hyper-[[neoliberal]], [[technological determinism|technologically deterministic]], anti-democratic, anti-egalitarian, [[eugenics|pro-eugenicist]], racist and, likely, fascist", and ridicules the entire accelerationist framework as a faulty attempt at "mainstreaming... misogynist, racist and fascist discourses."<ref name=Burrows>{{cite web|author=Burrows, Roger|date=10 June 2020|title=On Neoreaction|url=https://www.thesociologicalreview.com/on-neoreaction/|publisher=The Sociological Review|access-date=11 June 2020|archive-date=21 December 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201221004210/https://www.thesociologicalreview.com/on-neoreaction/|url-status=live}}</ref> He criticizes neoreaction's racial principles and for their brazen "disavowal of any [[discourse]]s" advocating for socio-economic equality and, accordingly, considers it a "eugenic philosophy" in favor of what Land deems 'hyper-racism'.<ref name=Burrows/>

== Relation to the alt-right ==
Some consider the Dark Enlightenment part of the [[alt-right]], representing its theoretical branch.<ref name="qz"/><ref name=warmed>{{cite web|title=The alt-right is more than warmed-over white supremacy. It's that, but way way weirder.|publisher=Vox|author=Matthews, Dylan|url=https://www.vox.com/2016/4/18/11434098/alt-right-explained|date=25 August 2016|access-date=15 October 2017|archive-date=31 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170831155255/https://www.vox.com/2016/4/18/11434098/alt-right-explained|url-status=live}}</ref> The Dark Enlightenment has been labelled by some as [[Neo-fascism|neo-fascist]],<ref name="qz" /> and by [[University of Chichester]] professor Benjamin Noys<ref name="qz"/> as "an acceleration of capitalism to a fascist point." Land disputes the similarity between his ideas and [[fascism]], claiming that "Fascism is a mass [[Anti-capitalism|anti-capitalist]] movement,"<ref name="qz"/> whereas he prefers that "[capitalist] corporate power should become the organizing force in society."<ref name="qz"/>

Journalist and pundit [[James Kirchick]] states that "although neo-reactionary thinkers disdain the masses and claim to despise populism and people more generally, what ties them to the rest of the alt-right is their unapologetically racist element, their shared [[misanthropy]] and their resentment of mismanagement by the ruling elites."<ref name=terrifying>{{cite web|url=https://www.commentarymagazine.com/articles/trumps-terrifying-online-brigades/|title=Trump's Terrifying Online Brigades|author=Kirchick, James|date=16 May 2016|publisher=Commentary Magazine|access-date=25 October 2017|archive-date=19 May 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160519120731/https://www.commentarymagazine.com/articles/trumps-terrifying-online-brigades/|url-status=live}}</ref>

Scholar Andrew Jones, in a 2019 article, postulated that the Dark Enlightenment (i.e. the NeoReactionary Movement) is "key to understanding the Alt-Right" political ideology.<ref name="Jones2019">{{cite book |title=Critical Theory and the Humanities in the Age of the Alt-Right |editor1=Christine M. Battista |editor2=Melissa R. Sande |chapter=From NeoReactionary Theory to the Alt-Right |author=Andrew Jones |location=London |publisher=[[Palgrave Macmillan]] |year=2019 |pages=101–120 |doi=10.1007/978-3-030-18753-8_6 |isbn=9783030187521 |s2cid=197950589 |url=https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-030-18753-8_6 |access-date=2020-06-10 |archive-date=2021-02-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214095600/https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-030-18753-8_6 |url-status=live }}</ref> "The use of [[affect theory]], postmodern critiques of modernity, and a fixation on critiquing [[regimes of truth]]," Jones remarks, "are fundamental to NeoReaction (NRx) and what separates it from other Far-Right theory".<ref name="Jones2019"/> Moreover, Jones argues that Dark Enlightenment's fixation on aesthetics, history, and philosophy, as opposed to the traditional empirical approach, distinguishes it from related far-right ideologies.

Historian Joe Mulhall, writing for ''[[The Guardian]]'', described Nick Land as "propagating very far-right ideas."<ref name="Mulhall2020">{{Cite news |author=Joe Mulhall |date=2020-02-18 |title=Andrew Sabisky's job at No 10 shows how mainstream the alt-right has become |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2020/feb/18/andrew-sabisky-job-no-10-alt-right-adviser |access-date=2020-06-11 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=2020-06-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200615204603/https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2020/feb/18/andrew-sabisky-job-no-10-alt-right-adviser |url-status=live }}</ref> Despite neoreaction's limited online audience, Mulhall considers the ideology to have "acted as both a tributary into the alt-right and as a key constituent part [of the alt-right]."<ref name="Mulhall2020"/>

== See also ==
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
* [[Authoritarian capitalism]]
* [[Intellectual dark web]]
* [[Natural order (philosophy)]]
* [[Neo-nationalism]]
* [[Paleolibertarianism]]
* [[Paleoconservatism]]
* [[Post-truth]]
* [[Reactionary modernism]]
* [[Right-wing antiscience]]
* [[Social constructionism]]
* [[Social Darwinism]]
{{div col end}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== External links ==
* {{cite web|last=Land|first=Nick|title=The Dark Enlightenment|url=https://www.thedarkenlightenment.com/the-dark-enlightenment-by-nick-land/|author-link=Nick Land}}

{{Alt-right footer}}
{{Fascism footer}}
{{Authority control}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Dark Enlightenment}}
[[Category:Dark Enlightenment| ]]
[[Category:Alt-right]]
[[Category:Reactionary]]
[[Category:Counter-revolutionaries]]
[[Category:Far-right politics]]
[[Category:Fascism]]
[[Category:Paleoconservatism]]
[[Category:Paleolibertarianism]]
[[Category:Political theories]]
[[Category:Schools of thought]]
[[Category:Monarchism]][[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Pages]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
